narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 (below the average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 4 (also under the average 5.53), middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 23 (well below the average 36.70), and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It calculated a 72.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which directly led to the rejection of your admission as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, as its lower-than-average level strongly signaled higher risk in the model's eyes, outweighing other elements. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA provided a counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success due to its alignment with typical strong performers. Your Year 1 decile ranking also played a notable role, contributing toward the failure prediction because it fell in the lower half compared to peers. Overall, while your full-time status matches the norm for 92.7% of students and your GPA holds steady, the standout low LSAT combined with early academic deciles tipped the scales against you compared to applicants who typically show stronger standardized test results and consistent rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw your profile as riskier than the average student's for bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 36.70, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or highlighting upward trends might also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, explore preparatory programs proven to lift bar passage rates, and reapply once you've addressed these key areas—your near-average GPA shows you have a strong foundation to build on. Persistence pays off for determined students like you.
